questions.description: Discuss the following features of the European Commission: (i) the mode of appointment of its President and other…
questions.text: Discuss the following features of the European Commission: (i) the mode of appointment of its President and other members; (ii) the standard of conduct required of its members; (iii) its role in the legislative process under the EC Treaty, including its own law-making powers, if any.

Answer part (A) [or]{.underline} (B). Do [not]{.underline} attempt both parts. (a)With reference to the relevant case-law of the Court of Justice, explain when recourse to Article 95 EC as a legal basis is justified. Why is it important to establish legal limits on the scope of Article 95 EC? (b)By examining the case-law, explain the sources, scope and legal status of the general principles of EC.law.Consider in particular the case-law of non-discrimination, fundamental rights and legitimate expectations.

questions.description: National Motorways Limited ("NM") is a private Irish company, whose… Damages, Directive.
questions.text: National Motorways Limited ("NM") is a private Irish company, whose principal shareholder is the Minister for Transport ("Minister"). By licence from the Minister, responsibility for the construction, planning, operation, maintenance and financing of all new motorways and expressways if given to NM, which is entitled to impose tariffs for the use of the motorways it designs, builds and operates. All Irish-registered hauliers are obliged to pay an annual 'user fee' per vehicle for the use of NM's motorways. Hauliers not registered in Ireland are not required to pay this 'user fee', but instead pay a 'trip fee' each time their truck use NM's motorways. Article 1 of a (fictitious) EC Directive obliges Member States to ensure motorway charges do not discriminate, directly or indirectly, on the grounds of the nationality of the haulier and/or the origin or destination of the vehicle. The Directive has not been implemented into Irish law, although its implementation date passed in January 1993. LS, an Irish haulier, is considering bringing High Court proceedings based on the Directive against NM claiming repayment of the difference between the 'user fee' it paid and the total 'trip fees' it would have paid had it been charged as a foreign haulier. LS claims that NM should be regarded as a state body. This is denied by NM, which relies on a 2002 Supreme Court decision in earlier proceedings concerning the period 1993 to 1999 brought by LS, where the Supreme Court held that the Directive was not directly effective and expressly refused to refer a question to the Court of Justice on the basis that it was "quite clear" that the relevant provision lacked the necessary precision to be directly effective. In 2003, the Court of Justice, in a reference from an Austrian court, held that the provision satisfied all the requisite conditions for direct effect. The (fictitious) Limitations of Actions (Amendment) Act 2008 restricts the time period in respect of which claims for the reimbursement of sums levied by the State or public bodies in contravention of EC law to one year preceding the initiation of legal proceedings to recover any such debt has now been brought into operation. In Irish law the limitation period for claims against the State remains generally six years. Advise LS on whether and in what circumstances in a fresh action: (i) it may rely on the Directive against NM; (ii) the High Court would be obliged to disapply the 2008 Act; (iii) it could obtain damages from NM and/or the State under EC law in the event it is precluded from bringing its claim for reimbursement; (iv) the breach by the Supreme Court in the earlier case LS brought could entitle LS now to damages from the State in respect of the period 1993 to 1999.

questions.description: Write a note on [any three]{.underline} of the following cases, setting out, inter alia, their core facts, the key…
questions.text: Write a note on [any three]{.underline} of the following cases, setting out, inter alia, their core facts, the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration and how those issues were addressed by the Court of Justice/Court of First Instance: (i) Case 26/62 Van Gend en Loos [1963] ECR1; (ii) Case 22/70 Commission v Council ("ERTA") [1971] ECR 263; (iii) Case C-167/02 P Rothley & Ors v European Parliament [2004] ECR I-3149; (iv) Case C-200/02 Zhu and Chen [2004] ECR I-9925; (v) Case C-411/05 Féliz Palacios de la Villa [2007] ECR I-8531; (vi) Case C-341/05 Laval un Partneri [2007] ECR I-11767; (vii) Case T-201/04 Microsoft v Commission [2007] ECR II-3601.

questions.description: Assess the compatibility of the following (hypothetical) Irish measures and practices with Articles 25, 28 EC to 30 EC…
questions.text: Assess the compatibility of the following (hypothetical) Irish measures and practices with Articles 25, 28 EC to 30 EC and/or 90 EC as applicable: (i) Excise duty is payable on intoxicating liqueur made in Ireland within four weeks of its release from licensed bonded warehouses, or, if it is not stored in such bonded warehouses, within four weeks of its delivery for retail sale. Excise duty on imported intoxicating liqueur is payable within four weeks of its importation; (ii) A statutory provision prohibits the affixing of 'tinted film' to the windows of mechanically propelled vehicles 'with the exception of lawful stickers and dark, non-reflective film to the goods compartments of good vehicles'. The government argues that the provision aims to prevent crime and ensure compliance with the legal obligation to wear seatbelts; (iii) A statutory prohibition on the towing trailers by mopeds and motorcycles. The government argues the measure does not hinder the free movement of trailers as they may be used for other purposes and that the prohibition is necessary to ensure road safety.

Answer parts (i), (ii) and (iii). Note: Each of the scenarios arises after the implementation date of Directive 2004/38/EC on the rights of citizens of the union and their family members to move and reside freely within the territory of the Member States. (i) Hans, a German national, has worked in the security industry in Ireland for several years. His application to become a trainee Garda was refused because of his non-Irish nationality and because he was 36 at the date of his application, the cut-off age being 35. A limited number of traineeships are open to non-Irish nationals, where for operational reasons recruits with non-Irish backgrounds and foreign language skills are required. In addition, no restriction has been placed on UK national applicants joining the force. The age restriction is applied across-the-board to all applicants without regard to their experience or level of health and fitness. Advise Hans on the legality of the refusal of his application under EC law. (ii) Mary, an Irish national, lived and worked in Belgium for several years before returning to Ireland in 2007, whereafter she met and married Aman, a failed asylum seeker from Afghanistan. The Minister for Justice, Equality and Law Reform recently served a notice of intention to deport on Aman. Advise Mary as to whether Aman has an EC legal right to reside in Ireland as her spouse. The Department of Justice argues that Directive 2004/38/EC does not apply as Mary is an Irish citizen resident in Ireland, who married after her return to Ireland. (iii) Dominique, a French national, has worked as a schoolteacher in Ireland for seven years. She was arrested and charged with possession of cannabis for the purpose of supply, but convicted only of the lesser criminal offence of possession of the drug. Dominique was sentenced to three years imprisonment and ordered to be expelled from Ireland upon her release from prison for 5 years. She has a two-year old child who is an Irish national. Advise Dominique on the legality of her expulsion under EC law.

Answer part (a) [or]{.underline} (b). Do [not]{.underline} attempt both parts. (a) A market study commissioned by the Government of Erse (and island Member State whose northernmost region forms part of another neighbouring Member State) of overcapacity in the Erse's beef processing industry recommended reducing the number of processors. Following this report, the major processors from the whole island formed the Beef Industry Development Society Ltd (BIDS), which formulated an arrangement ('the BIDS arrangement') whose aim was to reduce the total capacity of the processing industry by 25%. The arrangement provides that member processors desirous of leaving ("goers") would enter into agreements with BIDS in which they undertake to leave the processing industry, to decommission their processing plants and to respect a two-year non-compete clause ('exit agreements'). The exit agreements are also to include the obligations not to use land associated with the decommissioned plants for the purposes of beef processing for a period of five years ('restriction on use') and to sell the equipment used for primary beef processing to processors in Ireland only for use as back-up equipment or spare parts, or otherwise only outside Ireland ('restriction on disposal'). In return, goers are to be compensated by BIDS, with such compensation payments being financed by its members who are staying in processing ('stayers'). On being notified of the first such proposed agreement between a processor and BIDS, Erse's competition regulator informed BIDS that it did not consider the agreement to be compatible with Article 81 EC and sought a declaration from Erse's High Court that the agreement infringed Article 81 EC. BIDS has resisted the application on the basis that the agreement does not fall under the prohibition contained in Article 81(1) EC as having neither an anti-competitive object nor anti-competitive effects. Assess whether this agreement infringes the prohibition set out in Article 81(1) EC and, if so, whether it could satisfy the conditions required for exemption under Article 81(3) EC. (b) ATCE, the automobile and touring club of Erse, is a non-profit making organisation which, inter alia, in that (hypothetical) Member State organises motor sports competitions and concludes sponsorships, advertising and insurance agreements for such competitions. Furthermore, ATCE has been entrusted by Erse authorities with the supervision of national racing events and a statutory consultee of the Ministry of Transport of Erse in respect of applications for motor sports event licences throughout Erse from other bodies. FE-1 is a rival non-profit-making motor sports association whose activity likewise includes the organisation of motorcycling events in Erse. FE-1 has regularly found that its applications for licences to organise events are frustrated by delays on the part of ATCE in giving its opinion thereon to the Ministry and/or by negative opinions that normally result in Ministry refusals. Advise FE-1 as to whether it could bring proceedings to challenge ATCE's role on the basis of Articles 82 EC and 86 EC. In particular, consider whether ATCE would be likely to be considered an undertaking for the purposes of EC competition law and, if so, whether its dual role (organising events and participating in the licensing process for other event organisers) is compatible with Articles 82 EC and 86 EC.

questions.description: Analyse the role, power and obligations of the Commission under Article 88 EC in assessing potential State aid measures…
questions.text: Analyse the role, power and obligations of the Commission under Article 88 EC in assessing potential State aid measures notified to it.

questions.description: Analyse with reference to relevant EC Treaty provisions, case-law and institutional practice, the respective roles of…
questions.text: Analyse with reference to relevant EC Treaty provisions, case-law and institutional practice, the respective roles of the European Commission, the Council of the European Union and the European Parliament in the consultation and co-decision law-making procedures. Provide examples of legal bases applying each of the two procedures, and the typical voting requirements of each institution thereunder.

Answer part (A) [or]{.underline} (B). Do [not]{.underline} attempt both parts. (A) "It is axiomatic that the EC has only the powers ascribed to it, but defining those so as to impose meaningful limits on Community competence is difficult [...] The ECJ is often case as the 'villain' in this regard, by construing Articles 308 and 95 very broadly. It should be recognised that the greatest expansion of Community competence has been through successive Treaty revisions. It has been the Member States themselves that have been most willing to accord new competences to the EC" (Craig & De Burca, EU Law, 4^th^ ed., at p. 107) Analyse critically the above opinion by reference to the case-law of the Court of Justice on Articles 95 EC and 308 EC, in particular, and by reference to the successive amendments of the former EEC Treaty since the Single European Act. (B) Analyse the principles of subsidiary introduced into the EC by the Treaty of Maastricht (now Article 5 EC) and as developed in the Protocol to the Treaty of Amsterdam, and assess, by reference in particular to relevant case-law and legal literature, whether it acts as an effective check on the adoption of legislation by the Community in areas where it does not have exclusive competence.

Answer parts (i) [and]{.underline} (ii). (i) Analyse by reference to the case-law of the Court the procedural safeguards afforded to Member States during the pre-litigation phase of infringement actions under Articles 226 EC and 228 EC. (ii) Assess the view that Articles 226 EC and 228 EC, as interpreted by the Court of Justice, provide an increasingly effective instrument for securing compliance by Member States with their EC law obligations.

questions.description: Consider both of the following situations: (i) COMTEL, the Irish… Damages, Directive.
questions.text: Consider both of the following situations: (i) COMTEL, the Irish telecommunications regulator, calculates telecommunications licence fees in a manner that violates an EC Directive. The Directive has not been transposed into Irish law, although the implementation date has passed. COMTEL grants a private company, FE-1 Telecom, a licence subject to payment of such a licence fee. FE-1 fails to challenge COMTEL's imposition of the licence fee within the one-month limitation period provided under the relevant Irish rules, but has not yet paid the fee. Advise FE-1 as to whether EC law entitles it to refuse to pay the fee and whether it may challenge the legality of the fee under the Directive even though the one-month limitation period in Irish law has passed. Advise also whether it is entitled to damages under EC law arising out of Ireland's breach of the Directive. (ii) The National Roads Authority ('NRA') granted permission to build a motorway through an area of outstanding natural beauty. John challenged this permission in the High Court, but the High Court rejected his argument that the permission violated an EC Directive. The High Court also ordered that John pay the NRA's legal costs, which amounted to over €300,000. John believes that this High Court costs award violated article 10a of the Directive, which provides for access to justice in environmental cases "at reasonable cost". Ireland has not formally implemented Article 10a, but the Irish courts have a general discretion to award costs as they see fit. Advise John on whether he may rely on Article 10a of the Directive in his appeal to the Supreme Court and whether, on this basis, the Supreme Court must overturn the High Court costs award. Advise John also as to whether the Supreme Court must refer the matter to the Court of Justice under Article 234 EC. The latter has yet to interpret Article 10a.

questions.description: Write a note on [any three]{.underline} of the following cases, setting out, inter alia, their core facts, the key…
questions.text: Write a note on [any three]{.underline} of the following cases, setting out, inter alia, their core facts, the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration and how those issues were addressed by the Court of Justice/Court of First Instance: (i) Case 22/70 Commission v Council ("ERTA") [1971] ECR 263; (ii) Case 106/77 Amministrazione deel Finanze dello Stato v Simmenthal [1978] ECR 629; (iii) Cases C-46/93 and C-48/93 Brasserie du Pecheur v Germany & Factortame III [1996] ECR I-1029; (iv) Case C-112/00 Schmidberger v Austria [2003] ECR I-5659; (v) Case C-176/03 Commission v Council (Validity of Framework Decision 2003/80/JHA) [2005] ECR I-7879; (vi) Case C-127/08 Metock and Others [2008] ECR I-NYR (Judgment of 25/07/2008); (vii) Case T-201/04 Microsoft v Commission [2007] ECR II-3601.

questions.description: Assess the compatibility of each of the following (hypothetical) Irish measures and practices with Articles 28 EC to 30…
questions.text: Assess the compatibility of each of the following (hypothetical) Irish measures and practices with Articles 28 EC to 30 EC, 43 EC, 49 EC and Articles 56-58 EC as applicable: (i) A statutory prohibition on the towing of trailers by mopeds and motorcycles. The Government argues the measure does not hinder the free movement of trailers as they may be used for other purposes and that the prohibition is necessary to ensure road safety. Trailers designed specifically for mopeds and motorcycles require modification before being capable of being towed by other vehicles; (ii) A statutory restriction on the sale of herbal remedy for colds and flu, tincture of phantasm, without a doctor's prescription. The Government argues that the remedy may have side-effects on the operation of other prescription drugs, so on this basis, should only be available on prescription; (iii) A provision that allows a natural person living in Ireland to claim an exemption from income tax on dividends distributed in the form of shares in a subsidiary by companies established in Ireland.

Answer parts (i), (ii) and (iii). Note: Each of the scenarios arises after the implementation date of Directive 2004/38/EC on the rights of citizens of the union and their family members to move and reside freely within the territory of the Member States. (i) Heinz is an Austrian national who has recently moved to Ireland in search of employment. He applies for a job as a senior IT manager in the Department of Foreign Affairs, but is refused an interview on the basis that the post is reserved or Irish nationals. Ireland argues that EC law permits it to reserve some public service posts to nationals. Advise Heinz on whether this is so, and whether the exemption may be applied to the post in question; (ii) Jacqueline is a German national who moved to the Netherlands to study to become a special needs teacher on a four-year course accredited by an Amsterdam University and involving both periods of academic study and practical in-school training. Having initially been awarded a maintenance grant on account of being classified as an EU migrant worker, this was later withdrawn when, during her final year, she devoted herself full-time to study. The relevant awarding authority justified its decision on the basis that she could not, in fact, be regarded as a worker for the purpose of the grant having come to the Netherlands principally for the purpose of student and, furthermore, had not been resident in the Netherlands for five years prior to first applying for the grant, a prerequisite to being regarded as sufficiently integrated into Dutch society to be treated the same as a student of Dutch nationality. Advise Jacqueline on the legality under EC law of the withdrawal of her maintenance grant. (iii) Dominique, a French national, worked as a computer programmer in Ireland for seven years before being arrested and charged with possession of cannabis for the purpose of supply. She was later convicted of the lesser criminal offence of possession of the drug and sentenced to three years imprisonment and to expulsion and exclusion from Ireland upon her release for a period of five years. She has a five-year old child who is an Irish national and has commenced primary school in Dublin. Whilst in prison, she married Michel, an old school flame of Algerian nationality who came over to support her during her trial. Advise Dominique, who is due soon for release, on the legality of the expulsion order under EC law and of whether Michel is entitled under EC law to reside with her in Ireland as her spouse.

Answer part (A) [or]{.underline} (B). Do [not]{.underline} attempt both parts. (A) Assess, by reference to the relevant Treaty provisions, legislation and case-law, the significance of the contribution of EC law to promoting equality between men and women as regards equal pay and equal treatment in respect of access to and conditions of employment, including protection in respect of pregnancy and maternity. (B) Assess critically the operation of the new decentralised system for the enforcement of EC competition law under Council Regulation (EC) No. 1/2003 of 16 December 2002 on the implementation of the rules of competition laid down in Articles 81 and 82 of the Treaty which has been in force since 1 May 2004.
